intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Aider is an interactive AI pair-programming agent that runs in the developer's terminal on their own account and credentials, scoped to a single local git repository. It reads files the developer shares into the chat, proposes edits to satisfy natural-language requests, applies approved edits to the working tree, auto-commits them with a descriptive message, and can lint, run developer-authorized tests and shell commands (`/run`, `/test`, `/lint`), scrape developer-supplied URLs (`/web`), and read images or voice input. The remit leaves the developer in control: shell execution, commits, pushes, package installs, and edits to security-sensitive files require explicit human approval, and Aider must never push autonomously, write outside the repository root, exfiltrate code or secrets, or follow instructions embedded in untrusted file content.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python CLI application under `aider/`, organized around a `Coder` class hierarchy in `aider/coders/` (a `base_coder.py` orchestrator plus per-edit-format coders) driving developer-configured LLMs. The human-in-the-loop gate is `IO.confirm_ask()` in `io.py`; shell execution flows through `run_cmd()` (`subprocess.Popen(shell=True)` on an interactive shell) invoked by `commands.py` (`/run`, `/test`, `/lint`, `/git`) and by `base_coder.handle_shell_commands()` for model-suggested commands. Edits are applied via `apply_updates()`/`allowed_to_edit()` and committed by `repo.py`; untrusted content enters LLM context from file bodies, the repo map (`repomap.py`), git history, scraped web pages (`scrape.py`), and watched `AI!`/`AI?` comments (`watch.py`). Notably, file-write path resolution (`abs_root_path` → `utils.safe_abs_path`) applies no repository-root boundary, `is_important()` (`special.py`) lists security-sensitive files but is consulted only for repo-map ranking, and analytics (`analytics.py`) initializes PostHog with exception autocapture enabled.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is a genuine developer-in-the-loop confirmation architecture with real teeth on the highest-impact path but soft edges everywhere else. The strongest control is verifiable and holds: model-suggested shell commands are gated by confirm_ask(..., explicit_yes_required=True), which even --yes-always cannot auto-approve and for which a group "all" answer cannot be selected — so the classic external-content-to-shell-execution chain is blocked in one hop, and no autonomous git push capability exists anywhere in the code.

The gaps cluster in what the confirm gate does not deterministically enforce: there is no code-level repository-root confinement on file writes (path resolution is a bare Path.resolve(), and the one /add guard is a weak startswith prefix check coupled to auto_commits), --yes-always silently auto-approves package installs and edits to not-yet-added files, security-sensitive files are edited through the ordinary path despite an unused is_important() inventory, and no secret scanning or untrusted-content labeling exists before file bodies, diffs, or scraped pages reach the LLM and git history. These are the surfaces to review first.

rules.rule_text: Act as an interactive AI pair programmer in the developer's terminal: read source code the developer shares into the chat, propose edits to accomplish the developer's natural-language requests, apply approved edits to the local working tree, and commit them to git with a message describing the change — always leaving the developer in control and able to review or undo any change.
Running as a background service, daemon, scheduled job, or cron task — Aider is interactive and developer-driven.
Anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only; must never carry code, prompts, keys, or PII.
Arbitrary shell / `/run` execution of any command the developer did not author or that the LLM proposed — the exact command must be shown to and confirmed by the developer.
Including secret-like strings from the repository or `.env` in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages.
Committing, staging, or writing any secret-like string into git history.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Editing security-sensitive files: container build files (Dockerfiles), CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files (CODEOWNERS), and security-policy files.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Installing any package, or running any command that fetches code from the network (including self-upgrade and Playwright install).

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Committing to git (the developer must be able to review the diff before it is committed).
Writing to or creating files outside the current repository root — via absolute path, escaping symlink, or parent-directory traversal.

Following instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand Aider's capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.
Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Alert Operator (Do Not Halt)
rules.rule_text: Scraped web content, a watched `AI` comment, or other untrusted input containing directive-like language that tries to steer Aider beyond the developer's request.

findings.summary: File writes have no deterministic repository-root confinement; the boundary is only a bypassable confirm prompt and a weak prefix check.
findings.description: `abs_root_path()` joins the model-supplied path to the repo root and passes it through `utils.safe_abs_path()`, which is a bare `Path.resolve()` — an absolute path or `../` traversal resolves outside the root with no boundary check. `allowed_to_edit()` gates such writes only with a non-`explicit_yes` confirm prompt (auto-approved under `--yes-always`), and the one `/add` guard in `cmd_add` is a `startswith` prefix match coupled to `auto_commits` being enabled. The remit lists out-of-repo writes as never allowed, but the control is present-and-bypassable rather than a real confinement.
policy_rule_text: Writing to or creating files outside the current repository root — via absolute path, escaping symlink, or parent-directory traversal.

evidence.snippet: safe_abs_path() returns Path(res).resolve() with no repository-root boundary check
cmd_add out-of-repo guard uses abs_file_path.startswith(self.coder.root) (no separator boundary) and is gated on self.coder.auto_commits, so --no-auto-commits skips it

recommended_actions: Add a deterministic repo-root confinement check in `allowed_to_edit()` (base_coder.py) that rejects any resolved edit target not contained within `self.root`, using a proper path-prefix test (`os.path.commonpath` or `Path.is_relative_to`), independent of `auto_commits` and not satisfiable by the confirm prompt.
Apply the same boundary in `cmd_add` and replace the `startswith` prefix match, which passes sibling directories like `<root>-secrets`.

findings.summary: `--yes-always` silently auto-approves package installs and edits to not-yet-added files, which the remit forbids bypass modes from doing.
findings.description: Setting `io.yes = True` makes `confirm_ask()` return "yes" for every non-`explicit_yes` gate, so `check_pip_install_extra()`'s "Run pip install?" and `allowed_to_edit()`'s "Allow edits to file not added to the chat?" are auto-approved. Model-suggested shell commands are protected (they use `explicit_yes_required=True`, which returns "no" under `--yes-always`), so the asymmetry is real but incomplete. The remit explicitly requires that `--yes` / `--yes-always` never silently auto-approve package installs or out-of-repo writes.
policy_rule_text: Confirmation-bypass modes: `--yes` / `--yes-always` and non-interactive `--message` / scripted runs must not silently auto-approve pushes, package installs, or out-of-repo writes.

evidence.snippet: "if self.yes is True: res = 'n' if explicit_yes_required else 'y' — non-explicit gates auto-yes under --yes-always"
check_pip_install_extra confirms with confirm_ask("Run pip install?") — no explicit_yes_required, so --yes-always auto-approves the install

recommended_actions: Mark package-install and not-yet-added-file / out-of-repo edit confirmations with `explicit_yes_required=True` (or a dedicated bypass-exempt flag) so `--yes-always` cannot silently approve them, matching the shell-command gate.
Document precisely which gates `--yes-always` may and may not bypass, and add a test asserting installs and out-of-repo writes are never auto-approved.

findings.summary: No secret scanning or redaction runs before file content enters LLM context or before diffs are committed to git history.
findings.description: File bodies the developer adds, repo-map content, and full diffs flow to the LLM verbatim; `get_commit_message()` sends the entire diff to the weak model and the returned message is committed, with no detection of secret-like strings at any point. The `.env` default-ignore and `scrub_sensitive_info()` help only narrowly (scrub covers CLI args at settings display, not file content), so a secret hardcoded in an edited source file can enter context, a proposed edit, and git history. The remit forbids secrets in context, edits, commit messages, or history.
policy_rule_text: Including secret-like strings from the repository or `.env` in LLM context, proposed edits, or commit messages.
Committing, staging, or writing any secret-like string into git history.

evidence.snippet: get_commit_message() sends the full diff to the model and commits the returned message with no secret scanning
scrub_sensitive_info is applied only to the CLI arg line at settings display, not to file content, diffs, or LLM context

recommended_actions: Add an opt-out secret-scanning pass (e.g. a detect-secrets-style regex/entropy check) over proposed edits and diffs before they are sent to the commit-message model or committed, warning or redacting on a hit.
Screen file content added to the chat for high-confidence credential patterns and prompt the developer before it enters LLM context.

findings.summary: Untrusted content enters LLM context unlabeled and unneutralized across multiple ingress paths, with no directive-detection or alerting.
findings.description: File bodies, the repo map, git history, git commit messages, `/web`-scraped pages (added directly to `cur_messages`), and the code surrounding watched `AI!`/`AI?` comments all reach the model with no origin labeling, injection neutralization, or alert on directive-like language. An injected instruction can steer the model to propose edits that are auto-applied to in-chat files and auto-committed (see PRAX-2026-07-17-008); the shell and push escalations are blocked by the shell gate and the absence of push, so the chain stays reversible and in-repo rather than reaching execution. The developer reviewing diffs and confirming actions is the only mitigation.
policy_rule_text: Following instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand Aider's capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.
Scraped web content, a watched `AI` comment, or other untrusted input containing directive-like language that tries to steer Aider beyond the developer's request.

evidence.snippet: cmd_web scrapes a URL and appends the raw content to cur_messages as a user message, unlabeled as untrusted
process_changes injects the code surrounding AI comments (TreeContext) into the prompt; surrounding file content is untrusted but unmarked

recommended_actions: Wrap externally-sourced content (scraped pages, watched-file surroundings, git history) in explicit untrusted-content delimiters in the prompt and instruct the model to treat it as data, not instructions.

Add a lightweight directive-language detector that alerts the developer when scraped or watched content contains instruction-like text attempting to expand capabilities or override gates (remit escalation rule).

findings.summary: Security-sensitive files are edited through the ordinary path with no elevated approval, despite an unused inventory that names exactly those files.
findings.description: `special.py` defines `is_important()` / `ROOT_IMPORTANT_FILES` covering Dockerfiles, CI/CD workflows, dependency manifests and lockfiles, CODEOWNERS, and security-policy files — but it is consulted only by `repomap.py` for context ranking, never by `allowed_to_edit()`. Editing a Dockerfile or `.github/workflows/*.yml` in the chat is therefore applied and auto-committed like any other file, with no elevated confirmation. The remit requires human approval before editing these files; the declared inventory exists but is never wired to the gate that would enforce it.
policy_rule_text: Editing security-sensitive files: container build files (Dockerfiles), CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files (CODEOWNERS), and security-policy files.

evidence.snippet: is_important() classifies Dockerfiles/CI/lockfiles/CODEOWNERS/SECURITY but the only consumer is repomap.py filter_important_files ranking
allowed_to_edit() applies no is_important() check, so sensitive files take the ordinary auto-apply/auto-commit path

recommended_actions: In `allowed_to_edit()`, call `special.is_important()` on the target and require an `explicit_yes_required` confirmation before editing a security-sensitive file, so the existing inventory actually gates edits.

findings.summary: Analytics initializes PostHog with exception autocapture enabled, which can transmit file paths and code fragments beyond anonymous metrics.
findings.description: `enable_exception_autocapture=True` sends automatic exception reports whose stack traces can include local file paths, source-line fragments, and variable context — data outside the remit's "anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only; must never carry code, prompts, keys, or PII." Analytics is opt-in and model names are redacted (both positive), but exception autocapture is a distinct data-movement surface not scoped to anonymous metrics. The hardcoded PostHog/Mixpanel keys are public ingestion tokens, not user credentials, so they are noted by location only and are not a credential-exposure finding.
policy_rule_text: Anonymous, aggregate usage metrics only; must never carry code, prompts, keys, or PII.

evidence.snippet: Posthog(..., enable_exception_autocapture=True) — auto-sent tracebacks can carry local paths and code, exceeding anonymous-metrics scope
hardcoded posthog_project_api_key literal (public ingestion key, redacted here by pattern/location; not a user secret)

recommended_actions: Disable `enable_exception_autocapture`, or route exception reporting through a scrubbing layer that strips file paths, code fragments, and local variables before send, so telemetry stays anonymous and code-free.

findings.summary: `/web` scraping silently downloads and installs a pandoc binary with no confirmation or integrity check, and can disable SSL verification.
findings.description: When converting scraped HTML, `try_pandoc()` calls `pypandoc.download_pandoc()` to fetch and install a pandoc binary from the network with no developer confirmation and no hash/signature check — while the adjacent Playwright install path IS confirm-gated. Separately, the scraper honors a `verify_ssl=False` mode that disables certificate verification for outbound fetches. The remit requires approval for any command that fetches code from the network, making the unconfirmed binary download a divergence.
policy_rule_text: Installing any package, or running any command that fetches code from the network (including self-upgrade and Playwright install).

evidence.snippet: try_pandoc() calls pypandoc.download_pandoc(delete_installer=True) with no confirm_ask and no integrity verification
browser.new_context(ignore_https_errors=not self.verify_ssl) — SSL cert verification can be disabled for scrapes

recommended_actions: Gate the pandoc download behind a `confirm_ask` like the Playwright install, and verify the downloaded binary against a known hash before use.
Default `verify_ssl` to true and require an explicit developer flag (surfaced in output) to disable certificate verification.

findings.summary: Auto-commit (default on) commits LLM edits to git with no explicit pre-commit approval, only post-hoc review.
findings.description: With `auto_commits` defaulting to true, `apply_updates()` applies model edits and `auto_commit()` commits them with an LLM-generated message and no explicit pre-commit confirmation — the remit requires the developer be able to review the diff before it is committed. The gap is materially mitigated: pre-existing dirty changes are committed separately first, commits are attributed, and `/undo` reverts the last Aider commit, so every change is reversible and reviewable after the fact rather than silently persisted.
policy_rule_text: Committing to git (the developer must be able to review the diff before it is committed).

evidence.snippet: auto_commit() runs after apply_updates with no explicit pre-commit confirm_ask when self.auto_commits is set
--auto-commits defaults to True, so LLM edits are committed automatically unless the developer disables it

recommended_actions: Offer (or default to) a pre-commit diff-review confirmation for Aider-authored commits, or document that auto-commit trades the pre-commit gate for guaranteed reversibility via `/undo` so operators can choose per the remit.

positives.title: Human-in-the-loop shell gate that resists the bypass mode
positives.description: Model-suggested shell commands are gated by `confirm_ask(explicit_yes_required=True)`, which returns "no" under `--yes-always` and for which a group "all" answer cannot be selected, so each model-proposed command needs a fresh explicit "y" — blocking the external-content-to-shell chain.

positives.title: No autonomous git push capability
positives.description: The codebase contains no push call anywhere; pushing happens only via a developer-typed `/git push` or a developer-confirmed shell command, matching the remit's "Aider must never push autonomously."

positives.title: Default-deny of sensitive artifacts from watch/context
positives.description: `.env`, `.aider*` history/state files, logs, and caches are hardcoded into the file-watcher ignore list, and `.aider*` is added to `.gitignore` by default, keeping credential and transcript files out of the watched context surface.

positives.title: Analytics opt-in with model-name redaction
positives.description: Telemetry is disabled until the user is asked to opt in (`need_to_ask`) and model names are redacted to provider/REDACTED unless in a known list, so routine analytics do not carry raw model identifiers.

positives.title: Reversible, attributed edits
positives.description: Aider commits any pre-existing dirty changes separately before applying its own edits, attributes AI commits (co-authored-by / "(aider)"), and supports `/undo`, so any auto-committed change is reviewable and reversible.

rows.purpose: Records the developer's typed inputs and slash commands across sessions
Human-readable running transcript of the chat, including assistant output
Raw record of LLM requests and responses when enabled
Appends one JSON record per analytics event when a logfile is configured
Stores the analytics UUID and opt-in/disable state

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. Aider pairs a real, operative human-in-the-loop confirmation model — strongest on shell execution, which resists even the `--yes-always` bypass — with an absence of deterministic, code-level enforcement around it: no repository-root confinement, no secret redaction, no untrusted-content labeling, and confirmation gates that a bypass mode can silently defeat for installs and edits. The agent is well-scoped to a single repository and single interactive session with no autonomous push or daemon behavior, but its Zero Trust posture rests on a bypassable prompt rather than a boundary, and there is no verifiable adversarial-testing or security-grade monitoring evidence in the scanned source. The result is a coherent, developer-controlled tool whose safety depends on the human at the keyboard, not on controls that hold when that human clicks through.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Aider is structurally scoped to a single git repository and a single interactive, developer-driven session with a well-defined command palette and no daemon/cron mode, but its editing/shell action surface is inherently broad and the "single repository / no out-of-repo" boundary is enforced only by a weak `startswith` prefix check (`commands.py` `cmd_add`) rather than a real confinement.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Some real hygiene exists — `.env`, `.aider*`, logs and caches are hardcoded into the watch/gitignore default-deny list (`watch.py`) — but untrusted content from file bodies, the repo map, git history, `/web` scrapes (`commands.py` `cmd_web`), and `AI!` comments enters LLM context unlabeled and unneutralized, and no secret redaction removes credentials from context or diffs.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: A genuine, operative confirm-gate sits on the agent's path and is strong on shell execution (`explicit_yes_required=True`, unbypassable by `--yes-always`), earning real credit — but there is no code-level path confinement on writes (`utils.safe_abs_path` is a bare resolve), no input validation or output/secret filtering, and `--yes-always` silently auto-approves package installs and not-yet-added-file edits.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Self-upgrade from PyPI/main and Playwright install are confirm-gated (positive), but `/web` scraping silently downloads and installs a pandoc binary with no confirmation or integrity check (`scrape.py` `try_pandoc`); dependency pinning and any ML-BOM live outside the scanned scope (top-level `aider/*.py` + `aider/coders/`), limiting confidence.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The scanned source (top-level `aider/*.py` and `aider/coders/`) contains no verifiable adversarial-testing artifacts, red-team notes, or evidence that injection/confinement testing drove design; the confirm-gate architecture suggests threat awareness but no testing feedback loop is observable in scope.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Durable records exist — chat/LLM/input history files and an optional structured analytics event stream (`io.py`, `analytics.py`) — but these are UX transcripts and opt-in telemetry, not security-grade action logs, and there is no alerting on high-impact actions or directive-like untrusted input.
